label: 327_foreign currency_currency debt_currency_fxd
label_human: Emerging market corporate foreign currency debt
description: Non-financial corporations in emerging markets increasingly rely on foreign currency debt for financing. This trend has raised concerns about financial stability, particularly the risk of higher US interest rates leading to potential crises.

earliest_articles.title: The global financial crisis: Why were some countries hit harder?
earliest_articles.excerpt: -Country Causes and Consequences of the 2008 Crisis: International Linkages and American Exposure”, NBER Working Paper 15358. earliest_articles.title: Reserves and other early warning indicators work in crisis after all
earliest_articles.excerpt: With aftershocks of the recent global financial earthquake still being felt in some parts of the world, it would be useful to have a set of “early warning indicators” to tell us what countries are most vulnerable. Many scholarly papers in the past have been devoted to identifying leading indicators of crises (e.g.…

earliest_articles.title: What do we know about the causes of the crisis?
earliest_articles.excerpt: The Great Recession was the most important macroeconomic event in a generation. Policymakers are scrambling to guard against any repeat of these cataclysmic events. One strand of this effort is aimed at setting up an early warning system. The idea is to provide early warnings of macroeconomic or financial…

newest_articles.title: FX debt and optimal exchange rate hedging
newest_articles.excerpt: FX debt and optimal exchange rate hedging Summary Focus We examine the extent to which large companies around the world borrow money in foreign currencies and whether they optimally hedge the risks that come with changes in exchange rates. We use detailed financial data from thousands of firms in both advanced and…

newest_articles.title: New spare tires: local currency credit as a global shock absorber
newest_articles.excerpt: New spare tires: local currency credit as a global shock absorber Summary Focus Emerging market corporates saw a surge of dollar debt over the past two decades, potentially making them more vulnerable. We examine the ability of emerging market corporates to offset global shocks to their dollar debt by borrowing in…

newest_articles.title: From Carry Trades to Trade Credit: Financial Intermediation by Non-Financial Corporations
newest_articles.excerpt: From Carry Trades to Trade Credit: Financial Intermediation by Non-Financial Corporations We use unique firm-level data from Mexico to document that non-financial corporations engage in carry trades by borrowing in foreign currency (FX) and lending in domestic currency, largely in the form of trade credit,…

central_articles.title: Foreign currency corporate borrowing: Risks and policy responses
central_articles.excerpt: Non-financial corporations in emerging market economies (EMEs) increasingly rely on foreign currency debt for financing. Since the global crisis of 2008, the amount of dollar-denominated debt of EME corporations has quadrupled.1 Research has shown that interest rate differentials between EMEs and the US have…

central_articles.title: Global liquidity and dollar debts of emerging market corporates
central_articles.excerpt: A recent report by a working party of Robert Triffin International documented the major financial stability risks created by the post-Global Crisis surge in dollar liquidity (RTI 2019). The worry then (in December 2019) was the same as that of Acharya et al. (2015) – that an eventual rise in dollar interest rates…

central_articles.title: Corporate balance sheets in emerging markets: A comparison of the Global Crisis and the Asian Financial Crisis
central_articles.excerpt: In the aftermath of the Global Crisis, there was a rapid expansion of credit in emerging markets, accompanied by a surge in foreign borrowing and deterioration in net external debt positions (Acharya et al. 2015, IMF 2015). The non-financial corporate sector accounted for the lion’s share of this surge in leverage…
